distractors.text: Review with the stakeholders and make the corrections in the project charter.
distractors.trap: Consulting stakeholders still bypasses the sponsor's required approval.

distractors.text: Submit a formal change request to correct and update the project charter.
distractors.trap: A change request presumes authority the PM lacks over strategic realignment.

explanation: Strategic and business alignment is the sponsor's responsibility, so the PM should escalate the misalignment with recommendations rather than alter the charter independently.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
topic: Plan and Manage Budget and Resources
prompts: Organizational policy requires the project owner to be notified immediately of any budget changes. A project has used support personnel longer than planned, exceeding the allocated budget. What should the project manager do to manage the budget?

correct.text: Work with the team to find ways to lower costs, such as sharing resources across other projects.
correct.why: Optimizing costs through resource sharing addresses the overrun without simply expanding the budget.

distractors.text: Present the change control board an updated budget that accounts for the added personnel costs.
distractors.trap: Jumps to increasing the budget before trying to optimize costs.

distractors.text: Immediately stop using support personnel and replace them with existing delivery specialists.
distractors.trap: Abrupt reassignment risks quality and delivery without solving the cost issue.

distractors.text: Charge the support personnel's additional hours against the project's contingency reserve.
distractors.trap: Contingency covers identified risks, not routine resource overruns.

explanation: The best approach is to collaborate with the team to optimize costs, such as sharing resources with other projects, before escalating a budget increase.

eco_task: Manage Project Issues
topic: Manage Project Issues
prompts: An agile project is in its final iteration. At the standup, the team realizes it cannot finish all planned sprint tasks. The risk response strategy anticipated this situation, and a fallback plan was already developed. What should the project manager do next?

correct.text: Execute the fallback plan, since it is already part of the overall project plan.
correct.why: A pre-approved fallback plan is triggered and executed when its condition occurs.

distractors.text: Meet with the product owner to discuss the issue and the proposed fallback plan.
distractors.trap: Re-discussing a pre-approved plan adds delay with no added value.

distractors.text: Meet with the stakeholders to discuss the issue and the proposed fallback plan.
distractors.trap: The plan is already agreed; convening stakeholders is unnecessary rework.

distractors.text: Meet with the sponsor to discuss the issue and the proposed fallback plan.
distractors.trap: Escalating a planned response undermines the point of having it ready.

explanation: Because the fallback plan already exists and is part of the project plan, the team simply executes it when the anticipated condition materializes.

eco_task: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
topic: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
prompts: You are updating the organization's customer website to meet market needs. The backlog of changes is large and would take nearly two years to fully implement. What should you do to benefit the organization as quickly as possible?

correct.text: Rank the product backlog by business value and deliver low-effort, high-value items first.
correct.why: Prioritizing high-value, low-effort work realizes benefits soonest.

distractors.text: Revise the plan to enlarge the team so the total project duration is reduced.
distractors.trap: Adding staff raises cost and coordination without prioritizing value.

distractors.text: Assemble a globally distributed team so work continues around the clock to finish sooner.
distractors.trap: Compressing overall duration still delays any single early benefit.

distractors.text: Meet with the sponsor and stakeholders to establish a critical path aligned to business needs.
distractors.trap: Critical path shortens the whole project, not time-to-first-value.

explanation: Early benefits realization comes from ranking the backlog by business value and delivering the low-effort, high-value items first.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Project/phase Closure or Transitions
topic: Plan and Manage Project/phase Closure or Transitions
prompts: Your team has completed an agile project and the customer accepted all deliverables. The project faced many challenges that caused significant team conflict, and the team is still upset about the experience. What should you do?

correct.text: Conduct a lessons-learned session with the team to discuss what happened.
correct.why: A retrospective lets the team surface issues and identify improvements together.

distractors.text: Work with human resources to discipline the team members who caused problems.
distractors.trap: Punitive action damages trust and ignores systemic causes.

distractors.text: Meet individually with team members to express concern about their behavior.
distractors.trap: Framing it as personal blame avoids collective learning.

distractors.text: Document the conflict in the risk log, naming the members who caused it.
distractors.trap: Recording blame provides no resolution or improvement.

explanation: A lessons-learned or retrospective session gives the team a constructive forum to discuss what occurred and agree on improvements.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Quality of Products/deliverables
topic: Plan and Manage Quality of Products/deliverables
prompts: An organization hires a hybrid-experienced project manager to improve delivery efficiency. The organization currently uses a waterfall approach, completing a task before performing any quality analysis. How should you begin improving the processes?

correct.text: Work with the delivery specialists to learn the interdependencies and why the current processes exist.
correct.why: Understanding the current context with the team enables informed, collaborative improvement.

distractors.text: Keep current processes on active projects and apply agile approaches only to new ones.
distractors.trap: Leaving current processes untouched does not improve efficiency.

distractors.text: Assign one delivery specialist to own quality analysis on the current projects.
distractors.trap: One person owning quality does not improve overall efficiency.

distractors.text: Work with the PMO to develop process improvements for all active and future projects.
distractors.trap: Improving before understanding the current context risks the wrong fixes.

explanation: In a hybrid setting, the PM should first understand the existing processes and their interdependencies with the team before proposing improvements.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Scope
topic: Plan and Manage Scope
prompts: A company is launching an agile project. Stakeholders have accepted the iterative delivery approach and the scope statement. Your next task is to ensure the team clearly understands the work required. What is the most appropriate next step?

correct.text: With the team, build the WBS and work packages by decomposing the scope to develop the timeline and deliverables.
correct.why: Decomposing scope into a WBS gives the team a shared, detailed understanding of the work.

distractors.text: Create the project management plan, identify work packages, populate the schedule, and assign tasks.
distractors.trap: Jumps to scheduling and assignment before decomposing scope with the team.

distractors.text: Have stakeholders build the WBS and assign tasks with durations defined for each specialist.
distractors.trap: The team, not stakeholders, decomposes the work; this skips team ownership.

distractors.text: Build the communications plan first, then decompose scope into the project management plan.
distractors.trap: Prioritizes communications over understanding the actual work.

explanation: To ensure the team understands the work, the PM decomposes the agreed scope into a WBS with work packages, from which timeline and deliverables follow.

eco_task: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
topic: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
prompts: On an agile project, a stakeholder tells a team member who is their direct report that certain deliverables need to reach the customer sooner than currently scheduled. The team member relays this to you. How should you proceed?

correct.text: Meet with the stakeholder and product owner to understand the reasons for the requested delivery changes.
correct.why: Discussing with both parties surfaces the rationale and lets everyone review delivery options.

distractors.text: Meet with the stakeholder and cite the Pareto chart as the basis for the current schedule.
distractors.trap: Defending the schedule with a chart shuts down valid discussion.

distractors.text: Meet with the stakeholder and customer to discuss the updated delivery request.
distractors.trap: Bypasses the product owner who owns delivery prioritization.

distractors.text: Reprioritize deliveries to the stakeholder's request because of their customer relationship.
distractors.trap: Acting on influence alone skips validating the actual need.

explanation: There may be valid reasons for earlier delivery, so the PM should meet with the stakeholder and product owner to understand the rationale and review options.

eco_task: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
topic: Execute Project With the Urgency Required to Deliver Business Value
prompts: At the midpoint of a project, you discover a contract clause tied to local currency and the exchange rate. The local currency dropped in value, which caused the project to be stopped. What should you do to overcome the condition and execute according to the updated schedule baseline?

correct.text: Work with stakeholders to revise the project scope to fit the available budget and contract terms.
correct.why: Adjusting scope to the remaining budget is the only viable way to resume within the constraints.

distractors.text: Negotiate with the contractor to keep the project budget as originally planned.
distractors.trap: The budget is unchanged; the problem is the currency's dropped value.

distractors.text: Ask the executive sponsor to increase the budget so the project can finish.
distractors.trap: The project's stoppage signals the sponsor will not add budget.

distractors.text: Propose terminating the project.
distractors.trap: Termination abandons the goal of resuming delivery.

explanation: With the project halted by currency depreciation, revising scope with stakeholders to match the available budget and contract terms is the only workable path to resume.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Schedule
topic: Plan and Manage Schedule
prompts: During a project, the main contractor reports a delay caused by subcontractor performance, and further delays are anticipated due to external environmental factors. Which technique should the project manager use to bring the project back on track?

correct.text: Schedule compression
correct.why: Crashing or fast-tracking shortens the schedule to recover lost time without changing scope.

distractors.text: Resource optimization
distractors.trap: Balances resource use but does not by itself recover a slipped schedule.

distractors.text: Rolling wave planning
distractors.trap: A progressive planning technique, not a corrective action for delays.

distractors.text: Critical path method
distractors.trap: An analysis tool that identifies duration, not an action to recover it.

explanation: Schedule compression, through crashing or fast-tracking, shortens the schedule to recover delays without altering project scope.

eco_task: Integrate Project Planning Activities
topic: Integrate Project Planning Activities
prompts: During execution, an issue that was never identified as a risk causes a new installation to interfere with the existing site's infrastructure. What should the project manager have done to prevent this problem?

correct.text: Conducted a thorough risk assessment.
correct.why: Identifying the interference risk up front would have allowed a response plan to prevent it.

distractors.text: Communicated the risks to the project team.
distractors.trap: Awareness helps, but you cannot communicate a risk never identified.

distractors.text: Developed risk response plans for the identified risks.
distractors.trap: Responses only help for risks that were actually identified.

distractors.text: Monitored and updated the risk log throughout the project.
distractors.trap: Monitoring is useless for a risk that was never captured.

explanation: Because the issue was never identified, a thorough risk assessment up front is what would have surfaced the interference risk and enabled a preventive response.

eco_task: Integrate Project Planning Activities
topic: Integrate Project Planning Activities
prompts: As a new team leader, you want to build a high-performance team. Tuckman's model describes four stages of team development. Which is the first stage?

correct.text: Forming
correct.why: Forming is the first Tuckman stage, when the team initially comes together.

distractors.text: Norming
distractors.trap: Norming is the third stage, after conflicts are resolved.

distractors.text: Storming
distractors.trap: Storming is the second stage, marked by conflict.

distractors.text: Performing
distractors.trap: Performing is the fourth stage, when the team is fully effective.

explanation: In Tuckman's forming-storming-norming-performing model, forming is the first stage as the team comes together.

eco_task: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/methods and Practices
topic: Determine Appropriate Project Methodology/methods and Practices
prompts: On an agile project, an experienced architect argues that a detailed solution design document is needed upfront given the project's complexity. A team member counters that spending time on upfront documentation is against agile principles. How should you handle this?

correct.text: Allow the architect to create the solution design documentation upfront as the project requires.
correct.why: Complexity warrants the appropriate level of documentation; agile does not forbid it.

distractors.text: Provide agile training to the architect and work with the team to resolve the conflict.
distractors.trap: Frames the architect as wrong when the documentation is justified.

distractors.text: Agree with the team member and have the architect create the design in iterations over time.
distractors.trap: Denies the upfront design that the complexity actually needs.

distractors.text: Seek guidance from the sponsor since agile does not support comprehensive documentation.
distractors.trap: Rests on the myth that agile prohibits documentation.

explanation: Agile favors just enough documentation, and complex projects need more; the PM should allow the upfront design the project genuinely requires.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Schedule
topic: Plan and Manage Schedule
prompts: An engineering company is running a project to produce a product similar to a previous one. The component work packages have been defined and their constraints identified. Management wants the most precise possible estimate for the project's duration. Which estimating technique should the project manager use?

correct.text: Bottom-up
correct.why: Aggregating detailed estimates of lower-level WBS components yields the highest precision.

distractors.text: Analogous
distractors.trap: Uses historical data from a similar project, giving lower precision.

distractors.text: Three-point
distractors.trap: Gives an expected value and uncertainty range, not maximum precision.

distractors.text: Reserve analysis
distractors.trap: Determines contingency and management reserves, not the base duration.

explanation: With work packages already defined, bottom-up estimating aggregates the lower-level component estimates to produce the most precise duration estimate.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Project/phase Closure or Transitions
topic: Plan and Manage Project/phase Closure or Transitions
prompts: A project manager is wrapping up final activities as the project moves into phase closure. Which of the following activities does NOT belong to project or phase closure?

correct.text: Fast-tracking the schedule
correct.why: Fast-tracking compresses the schedule during execution, well before any closure activities occur.

distractors.text: Brainstorming team celebration ideas
distractors.trap: Recognizing the team is a genuine closure activity, not out of place

distractors.text: Reviewing critical success factors
distractors.trap: Confirming success factors is part of assessing closure

distractors.text: Conducting knowledge transfer
distractors.trap: Transferring knowledge is a core closure deliverable

explanation: Fast-tracking is a schedule-compression technique used during planning and execution to overlap activities, so it is not a closure activity. Reviewing success factors, transferring knowledge, and planning team recognition all occur during project or phase closure.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Project/phase Closure or Transitions
topic: Plan and Manage Project/phase Closure or Transitions
prompts: As a project reaches its final phase, the project manager plans the meetings needed to formally close it out. Which meeting is conducted during project or phase closure?

correct.text: Schedule and conduct a lessons learned meeting
correct.why: Capturing lessons learned at closure hands future teams knowledge to improve effectiveness.

distractors.text: Schedule and conduct a project kickoff meeting
distractors.trap: Kickoff meetings launch a project or phase, not close it

distractors.text: Schedule and conduct a change control meeting
distractors.trap: Change control meetings happen throughout execution, not at closure

distractors.text: Schedule and conduct a project status meeting
distractors.trap: Status meetings recur during execution, not at closure

explanation: Lessons learned meetings are held during project or phase closure to document knowledge for future teams. Kickoff, change control, and status meetings all take place earlier in the project life cycle.

eco_task: Plan and Manage Project/phase Closure or Transitions
topic: Plan and Manage Project/phase Closure or Transitions
prompts: A team is transitioning a completed product into operations and is working through the steps to validate the release. Which activity is NOT part of validating a release during the transition phase?

correct.text: Logging the deployment
correct.why: Logging the deployment is part of releasing into production, not validating the release.

distractors.text: Production deployment testing
distractors.trap: Testing in production is exactly how a release is validated

distractors.text: Active stakeholder participation
distractors.trap: Stakeholder involvement is required to validate the release

distractors.text: Stakeholder satisfaction survey
distractors.trap: Surveying satisfaction confirms the release meets needs

explanation: Logging the deployment belongs to releasing the product into production, not to validating it. Production testing, stakeholder participation, and satisfaction surveys are the activities that validate a release during transition.
